svg.text: src/gemm.c · which cells of A, B, C one column-tile iteration (j:j+vl, all 16 rows of the block) touches, for one l step
B: 1 shared vle_v load — C: 16 vse_v stores
A: 16 separate float loads, one per row
16 separate float loads — same column l,
1 × vle_v load — no teal slack here: unlike
opt_gemm, the load IS exactly j:j+vl, reused
by all 16 rows instead of reloaded per row
Amber = RVV vector instructions (B’s single vle_v load, C’s 16 vse_v stores) — vl elements moved per instruction. Blue = plain scalar float loads (A) — one element per instruction.

The mirror image of opt_gemm’s funnel: there, many B rows reduce into one C row; here, one shared B row (the waist) fans back out into all 16 C rows — no reduction tree because each row already owns its own accumulator.

Repeating this over all l accumulates every row at once; repeating over all j (rightward through B/C) covers the whole output — all 16 rows advance together, unlike opt_gemm’s one-row-at-a-time i loop.
see opt_gemm_blocked_dataflow.svg for the register view · cf. opt_gemm_arrays.svg for the k-unrolled single-row variant
